summary: The post explains how McDonald's franchise model is fundamentally a legal risk management strategy that shifts liability for worker treatment onto individual franchise owners while the corporation maintains control over virtually every aspect of operations. McDonald's uses surveillance systems to monitor revenue-to-labor ratios and 'encourages' franchise owners to cut worker hours, while contractual arrangements ensure worker complaints can't reach the parent company. The franchise owners are squeezed between corporate demands and worker needs, with labor costs being the only variable they can control. A recent NLRB ruling could allow workers to name McDonald's as a joint employer, potentially enabling unionization or forcing better franchise contracts.
key_points: 90% of McDonald's locations are franchises, designed to insulate the corporation from legal liability while maintaining operational control
McDonald's installs real-time surveillance tracking revenue-to-pay ratios and 'encourages' owners to cut hours when profitability dips
Franchise owners control only one variable—worker pay—but reducing it is the only way to stay profitable under McDonald's contractual demands
The franchise model transformed McDonald's from a burger company into a brand management and risk containment operation
The NLRB ruling could allow workers to hold McDonald's directly accountable as a joint employer
money_quotes: In order to contain your risks, you franchise.
Restaurants aren't the only business to learn that it's easier to sell and manage a brand than it is to sell and manage an actual product.

McDonalds essentially controls everything except one variable, but since it's a closed system of equations, that means the franchise owners have to decide between paying their workers reasonably and going in the red.
When did McDonalds stop being a burger joint?
It's a way to box in the legal risk of the parent company.
Their pesky legal issues are kept away from you.
Encourage, mind you, not require. They are not the employers or anything remotely like that, clearly.
key_insight: The franchise model is a mathematical sleight of hand that lets corporations control everything that matters while legally disclaiming responsibility for the workers who generate their profits.
